coredata.dc:title: Dual functional carbon nitride dots as electrochemical sensor and anticancer agent with chemotherapic and photodynamic effect
coredata.dc:creator: Thara, Chinnu R
Mathew, Sneha
Rose Chacko, Anu
Mathew, Beena
coredata.dc:description: We report the facile single-step synthesis of carbon nitride dots (CND) from l-ascorbic acid and urea as precursors. The as-synthesized carbon dots were water-soluble, displaying green fluorescence in UV light with a high quantum yield. Electrochemical studies manifest carbon nitride dots' excellent stability, sensitivity, and selectivity for mercuric ions and drug tetracycline detection. The former and latter's detection limit is 0.055 µM and 0.18 µM, respectively. Furthermore, cytotoxic studies with the carbon nitride dots exposed its selective toxicity towards cancer cells, which steered anticancer studies on B16 murine melanoma cells. These non-functionalized carbon nitride dots could induce cell death by cell apoptosis derived from chemotherapic and photodynamic effects.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nitride dots
Electrochemical sensor
Reactive oxygen species
Anticancer
Photodynamic effect
Cell apoptosis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical sensing of Mercury (Hg2+) and tetracycline (TC)
Mechanism of electrochemical sensing of Hg2+ and tetracycline
Anticancer properties of carbon nitride dot on B16 murine melanoma cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India
Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Chinnu R Thara (grant ID 09/499(0105)/2018-EMR-I) is grateful to Council of Scientific & Industrial Research (CSIR) for Senior Research Fellowship, and Department of Science & Technology (DST), Government of India for instrumentation facilities provided by DST-PURSE Phase II to Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, and Kerala, India.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and structure of carbon nitride dots. (a) Quasi-spherical carbon nitride dots (scale bar 2 nm). (b) Lattice resolved image showing d-spacing (scale bar 1 nm). (c) Particle size histogram. (d) XRD pattern. (e) FT-IR spectrum. (f) Raman spectrum of CND.
High resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, O1s spectra.

Electrochemical sensing of Hg2+ and TC. (a) CV run of bare GCE, CND/GCE, and CND/GCE/Hg2+. (b) CV runs of bare GCE, CND/GCE, and CND/GCE/TC. (c) DPV of CND/GCE with Hg2+ (1–6 µM). (d) DPV of CND/GCE with TC (1–6 µM). (e) Selectivity study of Hg2+. (f) Selectivity of TC.

Anticancer study with CND on B16 murine melanoma cells. (a) Anticancer study with non-irradiated sample at 12, 24, and 48 h incubation period. (b) Anticancer study with irradiated sample at 12, 24, and 48 h incubation period. (c) Effect of irradiation time.
ROS study. (a) Control. (b) 24 h incubated, non-irradiated sample. (c) 24 h incubated, irradiated sample. (d) Fluorescence intensity study.
Real water analysis for detection of mercuric ion (Hg2+) using CND/GCE by DPV.
Real water analysis for detection of tetracycline (TC) using CND/GCE by DPV.

head.ce:title: Dual functional carbon nitride dots as electrochemical sensor and anticancer agent with chemotherapic and photodynamic effect

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam 686560, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam 686560, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report the facile single-step synthesis of carbon nitride dots (CND) from l-ascorbic acid and urea as precursors. The as-synthesized carbon dots were water-soluble, displaying green fluorescence in UV light with a high quantum yield. Electrochemical studies manifest carbon nitride dots' excellent stability, sensitivity, and selectivity for mercuric ions and drug tetracycline detection. The former and latter's detection limit is 0.055 µM and 0.18 µM, respectively. Furthermore, cytotoxic studies with the carbon nitride dots exposed its selective toxicity towards cancer cells, which steered anticancer studies on B16 murine melanoma cells. These non-functionalized carbon nitride dots could induce cell death by cell apoptosis derived from chemotherapic and photodynamic effects.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals are one of the significant contributors to environmental pollution; they sometimes naturally occur in the earth's crust and most of the time by anthropogenic activities such as mining and smelting operations, industrial production and usage, and residential and agricultural use of metals and metal-containing compound [1,2]. Mercury is a recurrent environmental toxicant and contaminant that causes severe changes in bodily tissues and various adverse health effects. Because mercury is so prevalent in the environment, humans, plants, and animals are exposed to it [3–5]. Mercury severely threatens the heart, kidneys, brain functions, and other metabolic activities. The molecular mechanism of toxicity is based on its chemical activity and biological characteristics, implying that oxidative stress is involved in its toxicity [6,7].

In addition to the heavy metal pollutants, antibiotics are the steadily emerging pollutants [8] that pose equally adverse effects on ecosystem. The consumption of antibiotics has steadily increased over the decade in the medical field, livestock, aquaculture, etc. Excessive use of antibiotics leads to their unwanted appearance in terrestrial, freshwater, and marine environments. The presence of antibiotics in such unexpected places in considerable amounts resulted in the evolution of microbial pathogens with antibiotic resistance [9,10]. The increased prevalence of antibiotic-resistant bacteria and antibiotic resistance mechanisms in the environment refers to “antibiotic resistance pollution” having the potential to affect humans directly and ecosystem health. It’s exposure to humans is uncertain and epidemiological studies suggest that long-term antibiotic exposure can contribute to chronic diseases like obesity, diabetes, and asthma [11,12]. Due to the serious threats put forward by these pollutants, their timely detection in the environment is of at most importance. The traditional analytical techniques are either sensitive or selective but costly, or they are simple to use but lack sensitivity and selectivity. In this scenario electrochemical techniques are highly recommended among multiple sensing methods due to their numerous uses, including the detection of various chemicals such as pharmaceuticals, foods, biological components, and environmental toxins. Electrochemical methods also have the advantages of high sensitivity, ease of sample preparation, low cost, reproducibility, and the lack of organic solvents [13,14].

Due to their strong fluorescence and biocompatibility, quantum dots are also used in biological fields such cellular imaging, drug delivery, cancer treatment, etc. There is a critical need for appropriate diagnostic tools since certain diseases, like cancer, can significantly lower death rates with fast and accurate detection. There is now a lot of study being done on carbon dots (CD) in nanomedicine, particularly in imaging and drug administration, but there is less focus on its intracellular activity and possible anticancer processes. Carbon-based materials, such as carbon fibers, carbon nanotubes, and carbon dots have increased apoptosis and decreased cancer cell proliferation via various signaling channels. There are reports on some non-functionalized carbon dots synthesized from green sources exhibiting anticancer activity achieved by inhibiting proliferation, migration, and increased ROS generation in cancer cells [15–17]. Furthermore, carbon dots also show intrinsic photodynamic properties for photodynamic therapy (PDT). Carbon dots can transfer energy to surrounding oxygen under the excitation of a specific wavelength of light to form reactive oxygen species (ROSs), such as 1O2, O2.-, H2O2, and OH, which can kill cancer cells or cause them to apoptosis [18]. In the current work, we have used the traditional hydrothermal approach to create oxygen-doped carbon nitride dots from urea and l-ascorbic acid. One benefit of the synthesis approach was that the entire reaction, including the production of the carbon nitride dots and the oxygen doping, took place in a single step using inexpensive precursors under typical hydrothermal conditions. Carbon nitride dots had good sensitivity and selectivity for the electrochemical detection of mercuric ions (Hg2+) and tetracycline (TC). Further, its pH dependence, lower detection limit, selectivity, sensitivity, etc., were studied in detail. Cytotoxicity studies showed carbon dots peculiar toxicity for cancer cells, leading to an anticancer study on B16 murine melanoma cells. The carbon dots had dual anticancer action on cancer cell, chemotherapeutic and photodynamic effect. ROS study, imaging, and photoirradiation were also conducted to investigate the mechanism. To the best of our knowledge, there are no studies with oxygen-doped carbon nitride dots prepared from the precursors like urea and l-ascorbic acid. Additionally, we have successfully explored multiple applications with a single carbon nitride dot such as electrochemical sensing of Hg2+, tetracycline and anticancer study. Furthermore, the intrinsic dual mode cytotoxicity of carbon nitride dots for malignant cells is not noticed elsewhere. Thus, the oxygen-doped carbon nitride dot could function as a electrochemical sensor for Hg2+ ions, tetracycline, and. Simultaneously it could bring about cell apoptosis by chemotherapeutic and photodynamic effect.

Urea, l-ascorbic acid, sodium phosphate monobasic, sodium phosphate dibasic, sodium hydroxide, and hydrochloric acid, metal chlorides namely potassium chloride (KCl), mercuric chloride (HgCl2), copper chloride (CuCl2), manganese chloride (MnCl2), nickel chloride (NiCl2), cobalt chloride (CoCl2), ferric chloride (FeCl2), lead chloride (PbCl2), cadmium chloride (CdCl2) and chromium chloride (CrCl3), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) of analytical grade, were purchased from Merck (India). Drugs like 5-fluorouracil, doxycycline, tetracycline, amoxicillin trihydrate, cloxacillin, and vancomycin were purchased from Sigma Aldrich and used without purification. L929 (Fibroblast) cells and B16 (murine melanoma) tumor cells for skin cancer were initially procured from National Centre for Cell Sciences (NCCS), Pune, India, and maintained in DMEM and MTT assay (M−5655) (Sigma Aldrich, USA).

Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrometer (Japan) was used for recording absorption spectra. FT-IR studies were done using the Perkin Elmer-400 FT-IR spectrometer (Singapore) at 400–4000 cm−1. Raman spectra and lifetime measurements were recorded using the WITec alpha300RA (Germany) system with 532 nm DPSS laser and Horiba (USA) Fluorolog 3 TCSPC with LEDs of wavelength 295, 330, and 570 nm lifetime measurable from < 100 ps to 100 µs lifetimes. The structure determination was done with the powder X-ray diffractometer using Bruker AXS D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation of wavelength 1.5406 Ao. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were detected by Thermo ScientificTM ESCALABTM Xi+ X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer with monochromatic Al Kα radiation. Morphology and inner texture properties were analyzed using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) by a JEOL JEM-2100 microscope. The viability of cells for cytotoxicity was evaluated by an Inverted Phase Contrast Microscope and followed by the MTT assay method using a microplate reader (Germany). Fluorescence was imaged in a fluorescent microscope (Olympus CKX41 with optika pro5 CCD camera, Japan) and fluorescence was measured using a fluorimeter at 470 nm excitation and emission at 635 nm (Qubit 3.0, Life technologies,USA,).

Urea and l-ascorbic acid were taken in a 2:1 mass ratio and dissolved in 50 mL of double-distilled water. The mixture was stirred for half an hour to achieve a uniform solution. This solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and hydrothermally treated at 200 °C for 4 h. The brown solution was filtered through a microporous 0.22 µm membrane and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min [19]. Urea was chosen as the nitrogen source in carbon nitride dots due to its abundance, activity under thermal treatment, richness in amino, and, lastly, its cheapness. Ascorbic acid was picked as a new oxygen source instead of the usually used citrate salts. l-ascorbic acid had adequate number of oxygen rich groups; also it has antioxidant, and antibacterial properties, which could come into play during anticancer studies.

A three-electrode system in the Biologic SP 200 workstation studies was used to carry out the electrochemical studies. The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was used as the working electrode, where Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, and the Platinum wire was the counter electrode. The bare GCE was polished with 0.05 μM sized alumina slurry. A mixture of the sample and Nafion was drop caste on the electrode surface and dried at room temperature. The analysis was done at inert conditions at room temperature. The CV runs of bare GCE, CD-coated GCE (CD/GCE), and CD/GCE in the presence of numerous metal ions were recorded (Scheme 1 ). To determine limit of detection of analytes differential pulse voltammetry was utilized, for which different quantities of Hg2+ ions and tetracycline drug (1–6 µM) were prepared. The cyclic voltammograms of the modified electrode in the presence of Hg2+ ions and tetracycline drug were investigated at various scan rates. The EIS spectra of bare GCE and CD coated GCE were examined as well. Selectivity of modified electrode in presence of other metal ion and antibiotic drugs were examined with amperometric technique.

The L929 (Fibroblast) cell line was grown with DMEM supplemented with 10 % FBS, l-glutamine, sodium bicarbonate, and an antibiotic solution containing penicillin (100 g mL−1), streptomycin (100 g mL−1), and amphotericin B (2.5 g mL−1). In a humidified CO2 (5 %) incubator, cultured cell lines were incubated at 37 °C. A two-day-old confluent monolayer of cells was trypsinized and suspended in a 10 % growth media. To ensure sterility, the sample solution was filtered via a 0.22 µm millipore syringe filter. The seeded and grown cells were treated with varying concentrations of carbon nitride dot solution (100 µL, 50 µL, 25 µL, 12.5 µL, 6.25 µL) and incubated for various hours at 37 °C in a humidified CO2 (5 %) incubator. Non-treated control cells were also maintained. After incubation, the cells were checked for viability in an inverted phase-contrast tissue culture microscope and MTT assay method.

B16 murine melanoma cell line was cultured with DMEM supplemented with 10 % FBS, l-glutamine, sodium bicarbonate, and an antibiotic solution containing: penicillin (100 µg mL−1), streptomycin (100 µg mL−1), and amphotericin B (2.5 µg mL−1). A two-day-old confluent monolayer of cells was plated on 96-well tissue culture plates and cultured at 37 °C in a humidified CO2 incubator. After 24 h, the growth medium was removed, and freshly prepared compounds in DMEM (5 %) were five times serially diluted by twofold dilution (100 µL, 50 µL, 25 µL, 12.5 µL, 6.25 µL in 500 µL of DMEM (5 %)). Each concentration of 100 µL was added in triplicates to the respective wells and incubated at 37 °C in a humidified CO2 (5 %) incubator. Non-treated control cells were also maintained. The viability of cells was evaluated by an inverted phase contrast microscope followed by the MTT assay method. In the MTT assay method, fifteen mg of MTT was reconstituted in PBS (3 mL) until wholly dissolved, filtered, and sterilized. The sample material in wells was removed after 24 h of incubation. To the plate containing the test and cell control well, 30 mL of reconstituted MTT solution was added, gently shaken, and incubated for 4 h at 37 °C in a humidified CO2 (5 %) incubator. The supernatant was removed after the incubation period, and 100 mL of MTT solution in dimethyl sulphoxide was added to the wells, which were gently shaken up and down to solubilize the formazan crystals. A microplate reader measured absorption at a wavelength of 540 nm. The percentage of growth inhibition was calculated using the formula:
Viability
%
Mean
ODs
Mean
ODC
× 100

'ODS' is the optical density of the sample and ‘ODC’ optical density of the control obtained from the microplate reader. In vitro anticancer study was carried out with various concentrations of sample, various incubation periods, photoirradiation at multiple time intervals.

When the cultured cells reached 80 % confluency, it was treated with LD50 concentration of the samples (49.759343 µg/mL) and then exposed to light for 30 min, then incubated in the dark for 24 h control wells and un-treated control were also maintained. The cells were washed with PBS, added with 50 µL of DCFDA, and incubated for 30 min. After incubation, excess dye was washed with PBS and fluorescence was imaged in a fluorescent microscope, and fluorescence was measured using a fluorimeter at 470 nm excitations and emission at 635 nm.

Morphological studies carried out with HRTEM analysis show that carbon nitride dots have quasi-spherical structures. The lattice resolved images showed interplanar spacing of 0.31 nm and 0.263 ± 0.015 nm, which points to the presence of multiple active planes in the material and agrees with the characteristic planes possessed by most of the carbon nitride dots. The average particle is calculated as 3.11 nm (Fig. 1 a, c). The structure of the particles was confirmed with multiple analyses. XRD shows characteristic carbon nitride peaks, a major peak at 280 corresponding to the [002] plane, and a minor peak at 13.10 [100] [20] (Fig. 1d). Vibrational bands from 3400 to 3000 cm−1 corresponds to NH/OH stretching. Bands at 1570, and 1410 cm-1are attributed to the usual stretching modes of CN. The band at 2193 cm−1 indicates the presence of a cyano group in the structure. A major band at 1641, 1093.4 and a minor at 991 cm−1 represent CO and CO stretching vibration. 807 cm−1 band is the characteristic peak in carbon nitride compound that occurs due to out-of-plane bending vibration (Fig. 1e) [21]. Raman spectra show two bands centered at 1350 (D band) and 1580 cm−1 (G band) caused by the linking with N atoms and graphite-like sp2 carbon (Fig. 1f) [22].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed for the chemical state and constitution determination of the synthesized oxygen doped carbon nitride dots. The survey spectrum shows that carbon nitride dots are composed of oxygen, carbon and nitrogen with atomic percentages like 33.1, 49.3 and 8.07 %, respectively (Fig. 2 a). The high-resolution C1s spectrum consists of five peaks. Of these peaks at 284.5 eV and 288.2 eV corresponds to the standard reference sp2 bonded carbon of g-C3N4. The rest of them indicate 289.1 eV (COO–), 285.3 eV (N-CN), and 286.3 eV (CO) (Fig. 2b). N1s peak is deconvoluted to two peaks 399.9 eV, and 401.1eVcorresponding to pyridinic nitrogen (CNC) and graphitic nitrogen ((C)3–N). It might appear from graphitic C3N4 structure or the edge amino group (Fig. 2c). O1s peak is divided into two peaks 531.8 eV (CO) and 532.5 eV (CO) (Fig. 2d) [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical sensing of Mercury (Hg2+) and tetracycline (TC)
ce:section.ce:para: For the analysis, about 50 µg/mL of the carbon nitride dot solution was dispersed in Nafion solution (0.5 %). About 10 µL aliquot from the mother solution was drop casted on the GCE surface and allowed to dry naturally. Such a modified working electrode was used for further individual sensing experiments of Hg2+ and TC. The sensitivity of the CND modified electrode for the separate detection of Hg2+ and TC was determined with cyclic voltammetry (CV) experiments. Fig. 3 a and b clearly describe the enhanced sensing capacity of the developed sensor in the CV studies with the potential between −1.0 to 1.0 V with a scan rate of 50 mV/s. Bare GCE and CND modified electrode showed a weak cathodic peak at −0.26 V, which might occur due to oxygen reduction at the electrode surface. In the presence of Hg2+, a redox peak is obtained at 0.56 V and 0.031 V for the same modified electrode (Fig. 3a) [24]. The occurrence and variation of peaks in the presence of Hg2+ attributed to the productive interaction of Hg2+ to the CND on the GCE. Overall increment in the current response of modified electrode in the presence of Hg2+ suggests its sensitive sensing over CND/GCE [25].

A CV measurement in the presence of tetracycline shows a similar pattern of increase in current. The surface functional groups of CND bind with TC, resulting in enhanced anodic and cathodic responses compared to the bare and modified electrode. Also, the introduction of CND in GCE leads to an increase in surface area and high electrical conductivity, which cumulatively results in the sensing of TC [26]. Based on a few literatures, a range of pH buffer solutions was used in CV analysis to determine the appropriate pH for sensing of Hg2+ and TC (Fig S1, 2SI). For the metal ion and drug, the maximum current response was obtained for low pH values, and such behavior might come from the diminishing of acidic functionalities with the rise in pH. In the case of Hg2+ at pH 5, the maximum anodic peak was obtained, whereas, for TC, it was at pH 4. A scan rate study performed with optimized pH for Hg2+ and TC from 20 mV/s to 100 mV/s showed enhanced peak currents with an increase in scan rate (Fig. S2, S3). The limit of detection of Hg2+ and TC with the developed sensor was determined with the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) technique. For both analytes, the concentrations varied from 1 to 6 µM, and the feedback was increased peak current (Fig. 3b, c). Further, a plot of concentration of analyte versus peak current depicted a linear relation from which LOD is calculated as 0.055 µM for Hg2+ and 0.184 µM TC (Fig. S4, S5). Chronoamperometric technique from 0 to 200 sec was employed to evaluate the behavior of the modified electrode to possible interferences during the metal ion and drug detection. For the Hg2+selectivity study, a few metal ions (Pb2+, Cd2+, Cr6+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, and Ni2+) were considered potential interferents, and analyses were carried out in the presence of these ions at concentration thrice higher than [Hg2+]. Similarly, TC was selectively detected in the fact of interferents like vancomycin, 5-fluorouracil, amoxicillin trihydrate, doxycycline, and cloxacillin. Fig. 3d, e displays the selectivity of CND/GCE for Hg2+ and TC in the presence of interferents as thrice as the concentration of the respective analyte.

The reasonable use of thus fabricated electrode for the detection Hg2+ and TC in real water samples was checked with DPV method. Tap from Mahatma Gandhi University and Lake water from Alappuzha district, Kerala collected, filtered, and centrifuged to remove contaminants. These water samples at pH5 and pH4 spiked with known concentration of Hg2+ and TC analyzed by DPV method. Table 1 and Table 2 shows the excellent detection of Hg2+ and TC in the spiked real samples with RSD % of 0.003 to 0.40.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of electrochemical sensing of Hg2+ and tetracycline
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical sensing of Hg2+ with carbon nitride dot modified GCE involves mainly-two steps (i) ionic adsorption and (ii) redox reaction. In first stage the oxygen functional groups on the surface of carbon nitride dots on the electrodes surface acts as active sites for the adsorption of Hg2+. After adsorption Hg-intermediates are formed and the graphitic N atom in the carbon nitride dot promotes the charge transfer reaction resulting in oxidation and further reduction of Hg [27].

In the case of TC, the overall current response of the carbon nitride dot/GCE modified electrode increases in presence of TC, when compared to the current of bare GCE and modified GCE. It can occur due to the oxidation of some functional groups in TC, like the oxidation of the phenolic group to ketone. Similarly, at the optimized pH-4 TC can undergo protonation. These positively charged molecules can thus interact with negatively charged species like COO– and OH– on the surface of the carbon dot. Through this interaction, TC is electrochemically sensed [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anticancer properties of carbon nitride dot on B16 murine melanoma cells
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxic studies with CND on fibroblast cells and B16 murine melanoma cells showed exceptional selective cytotoxicity towards the cancer cells (Fig. S7). The viability of cells was evaluated by direct observation of cells by an inverted phase-contrast microscope followed by the MTT assay analysis. In the case of fibroblast cells, only a small fraction of cells have lost their viability even at higher CND concentration like 100 µg/mL. At the same time, more than half of the murine melanoma cells lost viability and morphology at a lesser concentration of CND (25 µg/mL) (Fig S8, S9).

Based on the cytotoxicity observations, anticancer studies were carried out with CND on the same melanoma cells. Carbon dots are known for ROS generation, and few reports show carbon dots anticancer properties by cellular ROS generation leading to inhibition and cell apoptosis. In the case of CND, anticancer studies were done in dark and light to know the cause of toxicity. Furthermore, the effect of the sample on cell incubation time was also studied in both conditions. From Fig. 4 a and b it can observed that the concentration and incubation time of CND has a direct effect on the cell viability. With a rise in CND concentration in cells reducing its viability to a greater extent. Also, from 12 to 48 h of incubation, more cell apoptosis occurs at 48 hrs. CND has a cytotoxic effect on the cells in light and dark conditions, even though in dark cells, the apoptosis rate is much lesser than with light irradiation. Still, in the absence of light, CND pose a potential threat to the melanoma cells with LD50 41.27 µg/mL for 48 h of incubation time (Fig. 4a). In the presence of light, more ROS could be generated as it can act as a stimulus to initiate the process. Thus, the LD50 of CND 48-hour incubated irradiated sample is 21.04 µg/mL (Fig. 4b). Cell apoptosis is further confirmed by morphological changes that occur to the cells at various incubation times and irradiation. Non-irradiated samples with 50 µg/mL of CND were obtained from an inverted phase-contrast microscope (Fig. S9). Effect of irradiation time studied at 15 and 30 min before incubation period (Fig. 4c) and 30 min of irradiation yielded more cell inhibition.

The intracellular ROS generations in B16 murine melanoma cells were further investigated using 2, 7-dichlorofluorescein di acetate (DCFH-DA) as an indicator. Analysis was carried out with both irradiated and non-irradiated 24 h incubated samples. Fig. 5 a–c show control with no fluorescence, and 24 h incubated sample without irradiation appeared green fluorescent, which occurs when the cellular generated ROS oxidizes the non-fluorescent carboxy-H2DCFDA. And the oxidized DCFH-DA emitted green fluorescence (Fig. 5b). The irradiated sample had more intense green fluorescence, probably due to the more cellular ROS production (Fig. 5c). The fluorescence intensity measurement of these DCFH-DA incorporated samples in a fluorimeter showed very low intensity for control due to the absence of cellular ROS. The irradiated cells fluorescence intensity was significantly higher than non-irradiated ones (Fig. 5d).

In summary, we have synthesized carbon nitride dots with a simple hydrothermal approach. The structure and composition of the material was studied using various characterization techniques. The CNDs showed excellent electrochemical selectivity and sensitivity for heavy metal ion mercury (Hg2+) and antibiotic tetracycline (TC) in the laboratory and real water samples with LODs of 0.055 µM and 0.18 µM, respectively. Investigation of the cytotoxicity studies revealed the exceptionally cytotoxicity of CNDs towards cancer cells compared to fibroblasts cells. Also, the material has dual-functional anticancer activity. That is, CNDs could cause cell apoptosis with normal chemotherapic effect and photodynamic effect. But of course, cell inhibition by photodynamic effect has more potential than the chemotherapic one. Thus, the one-step synthesized plain carbon nitride dot can act as an excellent electrochemical sensor for Hg2+, TC detection, and a potential anticancer nanomedicine.

Chinnu R Thara: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Sneha Mathew: Data curation, Visualization. Anu Rose Chacko: Data curation, Visualization. Beena Mathew: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Chinnu R Thara (grant ID 09/499(0105)/2018-EMR-I) is grateful to Council of Scientific & Industrial Research (CSIR) for Senior Research Fellowship, and Department of Science & Technology (DST), Government of India for instrumentation facilities provided by DST-PURSE Phase II to Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, and Kerala, India.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.108379.

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
